issues.description: File descriptor is closed out from under the caller. `File::from_raw_fd` takes ownership of the fd; when `writer` goes out of scope at the end of the function, `BufWriter::drop` drops the inner `File`, which calls `close(fd)`. The code comment itself acknowledges this: 'the caller expects the fd to remain open for further operations.' Any subsequent send/close on that fd by the caller will operate on a closed (or reused!) descriptor — classic use-after-close that can silently write to an unrelated socket/file if the fd number has been reused by another thread. This is a correctness and security bug.
issues.suggested_fix: Do not take ownership. Either (a) use `BorrowedFd<'_>` and a manual write loop, (b) wrap the File in `ManuallyDrop` so the fd is not closed on drop (e.g. `let mut file = ManuallyDrop::new(unsafe { File::from_raw_fd(fd) });` then `writer.into_inner()?; ManuallyDrop::into_inner` is NOT called), or (c) change the API to take an `impl Write` (or `&mut impl Write`) and let the caller manage the fd. Option (c) is strongly preferred — it removes `unsafe`, removes the platform gate, and is trivially testable against a `Vec<u8>`.

issues.description: The function calls `mesh.vertices()` and `mesh.triangles()` as methods and then indexes vertices as `&vertices[idx as usize]` with struct field access `v.x`, `v.y`, `v.z`. The actual `reify_kernel_occt::Mesh` (re-exported from `reify-types/src/geometry.rs:197`) is `pub struct Mesh { pub vertices: Vec<f32>, pub indices: Vec<u32>, pub normals: Option<Vec<f32>> }` — flat f32 arrays, public fields, not methods, not `Vec3`. There is no `.triangles()` accessor and no `.x/.y/.z`. This code will not compile against the type it imports.
issues.suggested_fix: Use the actual flat-array layout: `let vertices = &mesh.vertices; let indices = &mesh.indices;`. For each triangle index triple, read three consecutive f32s from `vertices`: `let base = (idx as usize) * 3; let (x, y, z) = (vertices[base], vertices[base+1], vertices[base+2]);`. Drop the `as f32` casts — the source is already f32.

issues.description: `&vertices[idx as usize]` will panic if any index in the mesh is out of bounds. In a streaming/IPC context this panics a worker and drops the client's connection mid-stream, leaving a corrupt partial STL on the wire. Export should return an error, not panic.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `vertices.get(base).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("triangle index {idx} out of bounds (len={})", vertices.len()))?` and validate bounds once up-front (`indices.iter().max()` vs `vertices.len() / 3`) before the hot loop to fail fast.

issues.description: `use std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd;` is Unix-only and is unconditionally imported. The reify workspace is built for macOS, Linux, and Windows (the Tauri GUI targets Windows explicitly). This will break the Windows build. The whole `export_stl_to_fd` path will need a Windows counterpart using `HANDLE` / `FromRawHandle` or — preferably — be rewritten to take `impl Write`.
issues.suggested_fix: Either gate the entire function behind `#[cfg(unix)]` AND add a Windows equivalent, or (strongly preferred) change the API to `pub fn write_stl<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, writer: W) -> Result<usize>` and let the caller supply whatever writer they need. The latter removes the platform concern entirely, removes the `unsafe` block, and makes the function trivially unit-testable.

issues.description: The SAFETY comment says only 'caller guarantees fd is valid and open', but `File::from_raw_fd` *also* requires that the caller transfer *ownership* of the fd to the `File` (so File can close it). The caller explicitly does NOT transfer ownership here — they expect the fd back afterwards. This means the unsafe precondition is actually violated on every call, not merely under-documented.
issues.suggested_fix: Either change the contract (use `BorrowedFd`, which has no ownership transfer), or document precisely that the function consumes the fd and the caller must pass a dup'd fd if they want to keep using the underlying file description. Resolving the 'fd closed on drop' blocker above will address this as a side effect.

issues.description: `(triangles.len() / 3) as u32` silently truncates for meshes with more than ~4.29B triangles. STL's format caps at u32, so any truncation here produces a silently-corrupt file that viewers will read as a smaller mesh. Should be an explicit overflow check.
issues.suggested_fix: `let tri_count: u32 = (indices.len() / 3).try_into().context("mesh exceeds STL's u32 triangle limit")?;`

issues.description: `triangles.chunks(3)` yields a shorter-than-3 slice on the final chunk if `triangles.len() % 3 != 0`. The inner `for &idx in tri` then writes only 1-2 vertices plus the trailing `0u16` attribute bytes, producing a corrupt STL with a mismatched triangle count and truncated vertex data. Also, the previously-computed `tri_count` would be wrong (integer division rounds down, but chunks still emits the partial).
issues.suggested_fix: Use `chunks_exact(3)` and assert or return an error if `indices.len() % 3 != 0` up front: `if indices.len() % 3 != 0 { bail!("mesh indices not a multiple of 3"); }`.

issues.description: No tests are added for the new function. Untested failure modes include: empty mesh, odd index count, out-of-bounds index, >u32::MAX triangles, write errors mid-stream, BufWriter flush errors, and the byte-count return value vs actual bytes on the wire. Since STL is a fixed-layout binary format, a round-trip test against a small hand-built mesh is straightforward.
issues.suggested_fix: Refactor to `fn write_stl<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, writer: W) -> Result<usize>` and add unit tests that (a) write a 1-triangle mesh to a `Vec<u8>` and assert exact byte layout (header, count, normal, 9 f32s, attribute), (b) verify the returned byte count matches `buf.len()`, (c) verify an out-of-bounds index returns Err rather than panics, and (d) verify an indices-not-divisible-by-3 mesh returns Err.

issues.description: The existing `export_stl` function uses `.context(...)` to annotate io errors with the target path. The new function propagates every write error with a bare `?`, so callers will see only 'Broken pipe' or 'Bad file descriptor' with no indication that the failure happened in STL export or which mesh/client. This is a regression in error observability relative to the neighbouring function in the same file.
issues.suggested_fix: Add `.context("writing STL header")`, `.context("writing triangle {i}")`, `.context("flushing STL writer")` on the relevant `?`s. When refactored to `impl Write`, callers add richer context at the call site.

issues.description: `bytes_written` is incremented by hardcoded constants (84 initial, 50 per triangle). This is correct today but is easy to desync if someone later adds or removes a field (e.g., a per-triangle attribute). The returned count is never validated against actual bytes written. For a streaming use case the caller likely cares about the real wire size.
issues.suggested_fix: Wrap the writer in a counting adapter (or track with `writer.stream_position()` if seekable, or — simplest — compute deterministically from `84 + 50 * tri_count` once, clearly commented, rather than incrementing in the loop). If refactored to take `impl Write`, callers who need a byte count can wrap with their own counting writer.

issues.description: `export_stl` (path-based) and `export_stl_to_fd` almost certainly share identical body code for header/triangle/attribute writing. Extracting a single `write_stl_binary<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, writer: &mut W) -> Result<usize>` helper and calling it from both entry points eliminates duplication, centralises the bounds-checking and divisibility fixes, and makes both call sites trivially testable. This also collapses the platform gate and the unsafe block to zero.
issues.suggested_fix: Introduce `fn write_stl_binary<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, w: &mut W) -> Result<usize>`. `export_stl(mesh, path)` opens the file and calls it; the fd-streaming caller constructs whatever writer it needs and calls it. Delete `export_stl_to_fd` entirely — the fd path becomes a caller concern.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The new `export_stl_to_fd` function has multiple blocking defects. Most critically, it takes ownership of the caller's raw fd via `File::from_raw_fd`, which means the fd is closed when `BufWriter` drops at end-of-function — the inline comment even admits this contradicts the API contract. Separately, the function will not compile against the actual `reify_kernel_occt::Mesh` type: the real struct exposes flat `vertices: Vec<f32>` and `indices: Vec<u32>` public fields, not `.vertices()`/`.triangles()` methods or `.x/.y/.z` on a vertex. The `std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd` import is Unix-only and unconditionally imported, breaking the Windows build. Vertex indexing can panic on malformed mesh data and crash a streaming worker mid-response. Additional suggestions: the triangle-count cast to u32 silently truncates, `chunks(3)` produces corrupt output on non-multiple-of-3 indices, error contexts are missing (regression vs. neighbouring `export_stl`), `bytes_written` is hand-maintained, the SAFETY comment omits the ownership-transfer precondition, and there are no tests. All of these collapse cleanly if the API is refactored to `fn write_stl_binary<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, w: &mut W) -> Result<usize>` — that eliminates the unsafe block, the platform gate, the fd ownership bug, and the duplication with `export_stl`, and it makes the code trivially unit-testable against a `Vec<u8>`.
